title: Dry-brush glaze technique
subtitle: Building a textured, streaky glaze surface with a barely-loaded brush over a base coat
excerpt: Load a wide brush with glaze, wipe most of it off on a paper towel, then drag the bristles lightly across a glazed bisque surface to deposit thin, uneven streaks. Repeat to build depth and texture. Works best over a contrasting base glaze. Cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Safety: mix glaze wet only.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first kiln firing (cone 06, approx 1000 degrees C) that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware
Pyrometric measure of heat-work in a kiln. Cone 06 approx 1000 degrees C, cone 6 approx 1220 degrees C, cone 10 approx 1280 degrees C
The second kiln firing that melts the glaze into a glassy coating and vitrifies the clay body; for cone 6 stoneware this occurs at approximately 1220 degrees C

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.
When mixing glazes from dry powder, always mix wet: never stir dry glaze powder in an open container. Decant glaze into a lidded container when not in use.

Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.

Dry-brushing is a glazing technique borrowed from decorative painting: load a wide brush with glaze, remove most of the glaze on a paper towel, and drag the nearly-dry bristles across the surface of a
bisque

-fired piece that already carries a base glaze coat. The result is a broken, streaky layer of the second glaze that catches on surface textures and high points while leaving recesses in the base colour. Applied over a contrasting or textured base, it creates the kind of surface variation that is difficult to achieve by dipping or flooding. The technique works on the
glaze firing
to
cone
6 (1220 degrees C).

materials.name: Bisque-fired stoneware piece with a base glaze coat already applied and dry
Mid-fire glaze (contrasting colour or texture to the base), pre-mixed

tools.name: Mop brush (for applying the base glaze coat)

content.text: When to use it

Dry-brushing works best on textured surfaces where the bristles can catch raised areas and skip over recesses. It is effective on thrown forms with throwing ridges, on hand-built surfaces with impressed or carved texture, and on pieces where a solid dipped base glaze would look flat. It is less useful on very smooth surfaces where there is nothing for the sparse brush load to catch on. The dry-brush pass is always applied over a dry, fully set base glaze coat: applying it to a wet base smears both layers together.
Applying the base coat

Apply two to three coats of the base glaze to the bisque piece using the mop brush, allowing each coat to dry fully before the next. The base coat should be opaque and even. Clean the foot-ring of any glaze.

Allow the base glaze to dry completely: the surface should be pale, chalky, and non-tacky to the touch. On a dry studio day, this takes approximately 20 minutes. In a humid environment it may take longer.
Dry-brushing the second glaze
Load the hake brush by dipping the bristle tips into the second glaze. A full load is intentional: you are about to remove almost all of it.

Wipe the brush on a paper towel with several strokes until the brush leaves only faint, broken marks on the paper. The bristles should feel nearly dry. This removal step is the defining action of the technique: not enough wiping leaves a wet brush that floods the surface; enough wiping produces the characteristic broken deposit.

Drag the nearly-dry brush lightly across the piece in one direction with the banding wheel stationary or very slowly rotating. The bristles should skip across the surface rather than laying down a wet film. High points and ridges will pick up a faint glaze deposit; recesses will show the base glaze colour.

Reload the brush and wipe again. Apply a second pass in a slightly different direction. Two to three passes, each with a freshly loaded and wiped brush, build the textured layer gradually. Do not overwork the surface: once the dry-brush layer looks right to the eye, stop. Additional passes add opacity until the texture effect is lost.
To keep the dry-brush layer on the upper part of the form and away from recesses, tilt the piece on the banding wheel and brush from the top edge downward.
What it looks like after firing

After firing to cone 6 (1220 degrees C), the dry-brushed layer melts into the base glaze where it was thicker, creating soft streaks of the second colour or texture across the surface. Where only a faint deposit was laid down, the second glaze thins to a wash. The two glazes interact slightly at their interface, particularly at the edges of the dry-brush passes, producing subtle transitions rather than hard lines. The texture of the underlying clay surface reads more strongly under this type of surface treatment than under a uniform dip glaze.
Variations
Three-layer build: after the dry-brush pass dries, add a third very sparse dry-brush pass in a third glaze colour for additional tonal range.
On thrown ridges: use dry-brushing specifically on the throwing ring ridges left visible on a pot. A metallic or iron-rich glaze dry-brushed over a pale base picks out the ridges distinctively.
Reversed: apply the dry-brush first, before the base coat. The base coat applied over the dry-brush layer buries the texture and reveals it only as a ghost under the surface.

rows.problem: Dry-brush layer looks like a solid coat, not streaky
rows.cause: The brush was not wiped dry enough before applying; still carrying too much glaze
rows.fix: Wipe on paper towel until the brush leaves nearly invisible marks on the paper. The brush should feel almost dry when dragged across your palm.

rows.problem: Dry-brush marks lifted the base coat
rows.cause: The base coat was not fully dry before the dry-brush pass was applied; damp base glaze is fragile and lifts under brush contact
rows.fix: Always wait until the base coat is chalky-dry to the touch before dry-brushing. In humid conditions, allow extra drying time.

rows.problem: No visible colour contrast after firing
rows.cause: The two glazes had similar fired colours, or both were translucent and the fired result blended to one tone
rows.fix: Choose glazes with a strong visual contrast in their fired state: matte against glossy, or a pale body glaze against a darker oxide-bearing second glaze.
